elements.sent-2411-001: The system under studying is for a piezoelectric beams system with magnetic effects, frictional dampings and source terms.
elements.sent-2411-002: We use the concavity method to study the competition of the dampings and the sources that leads to a blow-up result for solutions with negative initial energy.
elements.sent-2411-003: Keywords and phrases: Blow up, Negative initial energy, Piezoelectric beams, Magnetic effects, Nonlinear sources.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we are concerned with the following initial-boundary value problem \begin{equation}\begin{tabular} [c]{ll} $\rho\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha\upsilon_{xx}+\gamma\beta p_{xx}+\upsilon_{t} =f_{1}\left( \upsilon,p\right) ,$ $(0,L)\times(0,T),$ $\mu p_{tt}-\beta p_{xx}+\gamma\beta\upsilon_{xx}+p_{t}=f_{2}\left( \upsilon,p\right) ,$ $(0,L)\times(0,T),$ $\upsilon(x,0)=\upsilon_{0}(x),\quad\upsilon_{t}(x,0)=\upsilon_{1}(x),$ $x\in(0,L),$ $p(x,0)=p_{0}(x),\quad p_{t}(x,0)=p_{1}(x),$ $x\in(0,L),$ $\upsilon(0,t)=\upsilon_{x}(L,t)=p(0,t)=p_{x}(L,t)=0,$ $t\in(0,T),$ \end{tabular} \label{1}\end{equation} where $\left( x,t\right) \in(0,L)\times(0,T)$ for $T>0.$ The function $\upsilon$ represents the transverse displacement of the beam and $p$ is the magnetic current or the total load of the electric displacement along the transverse direction at each point $x$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The coefficients $\rho$, $\alpha$, $\gamma,\mu$ and $\beta$ denote mass density per unit volume, elastic stiffness, piezoelectric coefficient, magnetic permeability, and impermeability, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, the following relationship \begin{equation}\alpha_{1}=\alpha-\gamma^{2}\beta>0 \label{2}\end{equation} was considered.
elements.sent-2411-008: The nonlinear sources $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ are functions satisfying some conditions to be specified.
elements.sent-2411-009: Piezoelectric materials have the valuable property of converting mechanical energy to electro-magnetic energy, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-010: Piezoelectric actuators are generally scalable, smaller, less expensive and more efficient than traditional actuators, and hence, a competitive choice for many tasks, particularly the control of flexible structures.
elements.sent-2411-011: They are used in civil, industrial, automotive, aeronautic, and space structures.
elements.sent-2411-012: For more information we refer to $\cite{1 ,\cite{2 ,\cite{3 ,\cite{4 ,$ for instance.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: It is known in the literature that magnetic effects in the piezoelectric beams is relatively small, and does not change the overall dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, most models for piezoelectric beams completely ignore the magnetic energy.
elements.sent-2411-015: These models are known to be exponentially stabilizable by a mechanical feedback controller in the energy space.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, a beam of length $L$ and thickness $h$, the system $\left( \ref{3}\right) $ is for undamped Euler-Bernoulli beam model and the electrostatic or quasi-static assumptions describe the stretching motion as \begin{equation}\begin{tabular} [c]{l} $\rho\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha_{1}\upsilon_{xx}=0,\text{ \quad}$in$\text{ \ }(0,L)\times\mathbb{R}^{+}$ $\upsilon(x,0)=\upsilon_{0}(x),\quad\upsilon_{t}(x,0)=\upsilon_{1}(x),\quad x\in(0,L),$ $\upsilon(0,t)=0,$ $\alpha_{1}\upsilon_{x}(L,t)=-\frac{\gamma V\left( t\right) }{h},\quad t>0.$ \end{tabular} \label{3}\end{equation} Here, $V(t)$ denotes the voltage applied at the electrodes, and $\upsilon$ denotes the longitudinal displacement of the beam.
elements.sent-2411-017: The control system $\left( \ref{3}\right) $ is a well-posed boundary control system on a Hilbert space, with norm corresponding to the system energy.
elements.sent-2411-018: It has been shown that it is exactly controllable and hence exponentially stabilizable.
elements.sent-2411-019: In fact, by choosing the feedback $V\left( t\right) =-\upsilon_{t (L,t)$, the solutions of the system become exponentially stable $\cite{5}$, for example.}
elements.sent-2411-020: On the other hand, including fully dynamic magnetic effects in a model for a piezoelectric beam with voltage-driven electrodes.
elements.sent-2411-021: Morris and \"{O zer $\cite{6}$ used a variational approach to derive the differential equations and boundary conditions that model a single piezoelectric beam with magnetic effects given by \begin{equation}\begin{tabular} [c]{l} $\rho\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha_{1}\upsilon_{xx}+\gamma\beta p_{xx}=0,$ $\ \ \text{ \ }(0,L)\times\mathbb{R}^{+}$ $\mu p_{tt}-\beta p_{xx}+\gamma\beta\upsilon_{xx}=0,$ $\ \ \text{ \ }(0,L)\times$ $\mathbb{R}^{+}$ $\upsilon(x,0)=\upsilon_{0}(x),\quad\upsilon_{t}(x,0)=\upsilon_{1}(x),\quad x\in(0,L),$ $p(x,0)=p_{0}(x),\quad p_{t}(x,0)=p_{1}(x),\quad x\in(0,L),$ $\upsilon(0,t)=p(0,t)=\alpha\upsilon_{x}(L,t)-\gamma\beta p_{x}(L,t)=0,\quad t>0.$ $\beta p_{x}(L,t)-\gamma\beta p_{x}(L,t)=-\frac{V\left( t\right) }{h},\quad t>0.$ \end{tabular} \label{4}\end{equation} It is also worth to mention the work of Ramos et al. $\cite{7}$ in which the authors consider the system with a delay term acting in the damping of the displacement.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\begin{tabular} [c]{l} $\rho\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha_{1}\upsilon_{xx}+\gamma\beta p_{xx}=0,$ $\ \  \ (0,L)\timesR^{+}$ $\mu p_{tt}-\beta p_{xx}+\gamma\beta\upsilon_{xx}=0,$ $\ \  \ (0,L)\times$ $R^{+}$ $\upsilon(x,0)=\upsilon_{0}(x),\quad\upsilon_{t}(x,0)=\upsilon_{1}(x),\quad x\in(0,L),$ $p(x,0)=p_{0}(x),\quad p_{t}(x,0)=p_{1}(x),\quad x\in(0,L),$ $\upsilon(0,t)=p(0,t)=\alpha\upsilon_{x}(L,t)-\gamma\beta p_{x}(L,t)=0,\quad t>0.$ $\beta p_{x}(L,t)-\gamma\beta p_{x}(L,t)=-\frac{V\left( t\right) }{h},\quad t>0.$ \end{tabular} \label{4}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-022: Using an energy-based approach, they proved the existence and uniqueness of the solutions, the exponential stability of the solutions.
elements.sent-2411-023: For damping acting on the magnetic current $p_{t}$ with nonlinear source terms $f_{1}\left( \upsilon,p\right) $,$f_{2}\left( \upsilon,p\right) $ and external nonlinear sources $h_{1}$ and $h_{2 ,$ Freitas et al. $\cite{8}$ studied the system of the form \begin{equation}\begin{tabular} [c]{l} $\rho\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha_{1}\upsilon_{xx}+\gamma\beta p_{xx}+f_{1}\left( \upsilon,p\right) +\upsilon_{t}=h_{1},$ $\ \ \text{ \ }(0,L)\times T$ $\mu p_{tt}-\beta p_{xx}+\gamma\beta\upsilon_{xx}+f_{2}\left( \upsilon ,p\right) +\mu_{1}p_{t}(x,t)+\mu_{2}p_{t}(x,t-\tau)=h_{2},$ $\ \ \text{ \ }(0,L)\times$ $T$ \end{tabular} \label{5}\end{equation} They proved that the system $\left( \ref{5}\right) $ is gradient and asymptotically smooth, which as a consequence, implies the existence of a global attractor, which is characterized as unstable manifold of the set of stationary solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: For more results to piezoelectric beam with magnetic effects system in many different directions, we refer to $\cite{9 -\cite{13 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The system under studying is for a piezoelectric beams system with magnetic effects, frictional damping and source terms .
elements.sent-2411-026: The method used is the concavity method introduced by Levine $\cite{18 ,\cite{19}$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In the next section we present necessary conditions that needed to achieve our result.
elements.sent-2411-028: In section 3, we state and prove our main result, the upper bound of the blow up time.
elements.sent-2411-029: While in sections 4 we estimate the lower bound of that time.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this section we prepare some material needed in the proof of our main result.
elements.sent-2411-031: For this reason we assume that (G1) There exists a function $I(\upsilon,p)\geq0$ such that \begin{equation}\frac{\partial I}{\partial\upsilon}=f_{1}(\upsilon,p), \ \ \ \  \frac{\partial I}{\partial p}=f_{2}(\upsilon,p).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: Note that condition (G3) is necessary for the existence of a local solution to $\left( \ref{1}\right) $.
elements.sent-2411-033: As an example of a function that satisfying the other conditions (G1) and (G2) is \begin{equation}I(\upsilon,p):=\frac{a}{\eta}\left\vert \upsilon-p\right\vert ^{\eta},\end{equation} where we have \begin{equation}\frac{\partial I}{\partial\upsilon}=f_{1}(\upsilon,p)=a\left\vert \upsilon-p\right\vert ^{\eta-2}(\upsilon-p),\ \ \ \ \ \ \frac{\partial I}{\partial p}=f_{2}(\upsilon,p)=-a\left\vert \upsilon-p\right\vert ^{\eta -2}(\upsilon-p).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: We define the Energy space to be \begin{equation}H=\left\{ H^{2}\left( 0,L\right) \cap H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H^{2}\left( 0,L\right) \cap H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \right\} ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) =\left\{ g\in H^{1}\left( 0,L\right) ; g\left( 0\right) =g_{x}\left( L\right) =0\right\} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}H=\left\{ H^{2}\left( 0,L\right) \cap H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H^{2}\left( 0,L\right) \cap H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \times H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \right\} ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: The existence theorem can be stated as.
elements.sent-2411-036: For any $\left( \upsilon_{0 ,\upsilon_{1 ,p_{0 ,p_{1}\right) \in H,$\ there is a unique strong solution of the system $\left( \ref{1}\right) $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The proof can be established using the semigroup theory by combining the results in $\cite{6 ,$ $\cite{14}$ and $\cite{20 .$ To make the system $\left( \ref{1}\right) $ more convenient, we will divide the first equation by $\rho$ and the second one by $\mu$ but we keep the other coefficients with the same name.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The new system $\left( \ref{1}\right) $ becomes like \begin{equation}\left\{ \begin{tabular} [c]{ll} $\upsilon_{tt}-\alpha\upsilon_{xx}+\gamma\beta p_{xx}+\lambda_{1}\upsilon _{t}=f_{1}\left( \upsilon,p\right) ,$ $(0,L)\times(0,T),$ $p_{tt}-\beta p_{xx}+\gamma\beta\upsilon_{xx}+\lambda_{2}p_{t}=f_{2}\left( \upsilon,p\right) ,$ $(0,L)\times(0,T),$ \end{tabular} \right. \label{P}\end{equation} together with the same initial and boundary conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The energy functional associated to the above system is given by, for $t\geq0,$ \begin{equation}E(t):=\frac{1}{2}\left( \alpha_{1}||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2}+\beta ||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2}^{2}+||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t} ||_{2}^{2}\right) -\int_{0}^{L}I(\upsilon,p)dx, \label{6}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{1}$ defined in $\left( \ref{2}\right) $.
elements.sent-2411-040: Consequently, we present the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-042: Multiplying equation $\left( \ref{P}\right) _{1}$ by $\upsilon_{t ,$ equation $\left( \ref{P}\right) _{2}$ by $p_{t}$ and integrating by parts considering the boundary conditions over $(0,L)$ then summing up we get \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dt}\left[ \left( \alpha-\beta\gamma^{2}\right) ||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2}+\beta||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2}^{2} +||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t}||_{2}^{2}\right] -\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}f_{1}+p_{t}f_{2}\right) dx =-\lambda_{1}||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}-\lambda_{2}||p_{t}||_{2}^{2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Finally, we define \begin{equation}F(t):=\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \left\vert \upsilon(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}+\left\vert p(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}L\left( t\right) +\frac{1}{2}b(t+t_{0})^{2}, \label{8}\end{equation} for $t_{0 >0$ and $b>0$ to be chosen later and \begin{equation}L\left( t\right) :=\int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dxds +\left( T-t\right) \int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon _{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}L\left( t\right) :=\int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dxds +\left( T-t\right) \int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon _{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: For the functional $L\left( t\right) $, we have the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-047: Along the solution of $\left( \ref{P}\right) $, the functional $L\left( t\right) $\ satisfies \begin{equation}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) :=2\int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda _{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t}(x,s)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,s)\right) dxds.\end{equation}. $and$ \begin{equation}L^{\prime\prime}\left( t\right) :=2\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1} \upsilon\upsilon_{t}(x,t)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,t)\right) dx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: By a direct differentiation, we obtain \begin{equation}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) =\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx-\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon _{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx =\int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{t}\frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left[ \left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}\right) \right] dx =2\int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon _{t}(x,s)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,s)\right) dsdx.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) =\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx-\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon _{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx =\int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{t}\frac{\partial}{\partial s}\left[ \left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,s)\right\vert ^{2}\right) \right] dx =2\int_{0}^{L}\int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon _{t}(x,s)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,s)\right) dsdx.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: The derivative of $L\left( t\right) $ again gives \begin{equation}L^{\prime\prime}\left( t\right) =\frac{d}{dt}\left[ \int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx-\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx\right] =2\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t}(x,t)+\lambda _{2}pp_{t}(x,t)\right) dx.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}L^{\prime\prime}\left( t\right) =\frac{d}{dt}\left[ \int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p(x,t)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx-\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\left\vert \upsilon_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left\vert p_{0}(x)\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx\right] =2\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t}(x,t)+\lambda _{2}pp_{t}(x,t)\right) dx.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this section we state and prove our main result.\newline.
elements.sent-2411-052: Then for any initial data \begin{equation}(\upsilon_{0},p_{0},\upsilon_{1},p_{1})\in\left( H^{2}\left( 0,L\right) \cap H_{\ast}^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \right) ^{2}\times\left( H_{\ast} ^{1}\left( 0,L\right) \right) ^{2},\end{equation} \ \ satisfying \begin{equation}E(0)=\frac{1}{2}\left( \alpha_{1}||\upsilon_{0x}||_{2}^{2}+\beta ||\gamma\upsilon_{0x}-p_{0x}||_{2}^{2}+||\upsilon_{1}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{1} ||_{2}^{2}\right) -\int_{0}^{L}I(\upsilon_{0},p_{0})dx<0,\end{equation} the corresponding solution of $\left( \ref{P}\right) $\ blows up in a finite time.
elements.sent-2411-053: Before the proof, we need the following technical lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-054: Along the solution of $\left( \ref{P}\right) $, we estimate \begin{equation}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \rho\upsilon\upsilon_{tt}+\mu pp_{tt}\right) dx \geq\alpha_{1}\left( \frac{\eta}{2}-1\right) \left\Vert \upsilon _{x}\right\Vert _{2}^{2}+\beta\left( \frac{\eta}{2}-1\right) ||\gamma \upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2}^{2}\nonumber -\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t}+\lambda_{2} pp_{t}\right) dx+\frac{\eta}{2}\left( ||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t} ||_{2}^{2}\right) \nonumber -\eta E(t). \label{9}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: Multiply the equations in $\left( \ref{P}\right) _{1}$ by $\upsilon,$ equation $\left( \ref{P}\right) _{2}$ by $p$ and integrate by parts over $\left( 0,L\right) $ to get \begin{equation}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{tt}+pp_{tt}\right) dx =-\alpha _{1}\left\Vert \upsilon_{x}\right\Vert ^{2}-\beta||\gamma\upsilon_{x} -p_{x}||_{2}^{2}-\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t} +\lambda_{2}pp_{t}\right) dx +\int_{0}^{L}[\upsilon f_{1}(\upsilon,p)+pf_{2}(\upsilon,p)]dx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: Exploiting $\left( \ref{6}\right) $, we find \begin{equation}\eta\int_{0}^{L}I(\upsilon,p)dx=-\eta E(t)+\frac{\eta}{2}\left[ \alpha _{1}||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2}+\beta||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2} ^{2}+||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t}||_{2}^{2}\right] ,\end{equation} to reach the result \begin{equation}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{tt}+pp_{tt}\right) dx \geq \alpha_{1}\left( \frac{\eta}{2}-1\right) ||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2} +\beta\left( \frac{\eta}{2}-1\right) ||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2}^{2} -\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon_{t}+\lambda_{2} pp_{t}\right) dx+\frac{\eta}{2}\left( ||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t} ||_{2}^{2}\right) -\eta E(t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: By using Young's inequality, we have \begin{equation}I =\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+2\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0} )\right) \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx +\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2} \leq\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+2\left[ \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+\frac{1}{2\varepsilon }\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2}\right] +\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2} \leq(1+\varepsilon)\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t} +pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+(1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon})\left( \frac{1} {2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}I =\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+2\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0} )\right) \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx +\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2} \leq\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+2\left[ \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+\frac{1}{2\varepsilon }\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2}\right] +\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2} \leq(1+\varepsilon)\left( \int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t} +pp_{t}\right) dx\right) ^{2}+(1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon})\left( \frac{1} {2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0})\right) ^{2}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: Using Cauchy-Schwartz inequality yields \begin{equation}I \leq(1+\varepsilon)\left( \int_{0}^{L}\upsilon^{2}dx+\int_{0}^{L} p^{2}dx\right) \left( \int_{0}^{L}\upsilon_{t}^{2}dx+\int_{0}^{L}p_{t} ^{2}dx\right) +2(1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon})\left[ \left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) \right) ^{2}+b^{2}(t+t_{0})^{2}\right] .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: Using Lemma 2.2, we estimate \begin{equation}\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) \right) ^{2} =\left( \int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon _{t}(x,s)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,s)\right) dxds\right) ^{2} =\left( \int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}||\upsilon\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{2}||p\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}\right) ds\right) \left( \int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}||\upsilon_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{2}||p_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}\right) ds\right) .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) \right) ^{2} =\left( \int_{0}^{t}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \lambda_{1}\upsilon\upsilon _{t}(x,s)+\lambda_{2}pp_{t}(x,s)\right) dxds\right) ^{2} =\left( \int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}||\upsilon\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{2}||p\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}\right) ds\right) \left( \int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}||\upsilon_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{2}||p_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}\right) ds\right) .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: Using $\left( \ref{7}\right) ,$ we find \begin{equation}\int_{0}^{t}\left( \lambda_{1}||\upsilon_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2} ^{2}+\lambda_{2}||p_{t}\left( s\right) ||_{2}^{2}\right) ds\leq-E\left( t\right) ,\end{equation} together with $\left( \ref{8}\right) $ imply that \begin{equation}\left( \frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) \right) ^{2}\leq-2\lambda F(t)E\left( t\right) .\end{equation}. Hence \begin{equation}I\leq F(t)\left[ (1+\varepsilon)\left( \int_{0}^{L}\upsilon_{t}^{2} dx+\int_{0}^{L}p_{t}^{2}dx\right) +2(1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon})\left( b-2\lambda E\left( t\right) \right) \right] .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: From $\left( \ref{7}\right) $, we notice that \begin{equation}E(t)\leq E(0)\leq0. \label{11}\end{equation} By differentiating $F$ defined in $\left( \ref{8}\right) $ twice we get \begin{equation}F^{\prime}(t)=\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{t}+pp_{t}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}L^{\prime}\left( t\right) +b(t+t_{0}) \label{12}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}F^{\prime\prime}(t)=\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon\upsilon_{tt}+pp_{tt}\right) dx+\int_{0}^{L}\left( \left\vert \upsilon_{t}\right\vert ^{2}+\left\vert p_{t}\right\vert ^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}L^{\prime\prime}\left( t\right) +b. \label{13}\end{equation} Using the estimation in $\left( \ref{9}\right) $, yields \begin{equation}F^{\prime\prime}(t) \geq\alpha_{1}\left( \frac{\eta}{2}-1\right) \left\Vert \upsilon_{x}\right\Vert _{2}^{2}++\beta\left( \frac{\eta} {2}-1\right) ||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2}^{2}\label{14} +\left( \frac{\eta}{2}+1\right) \left\Vert \upsilon_{t}\right\Vert _{2}^{2}+\left( \frac{\eta}{2}+1\right) \left\Vert p_{t}\right\Vert _{2} ^{2}-\eta E\left( t\right) +b.\nonumber\end{equation} We then define \begin{equation}G(t):=F^{-\sigma}(t), \label{15}\end{equation} for $\sigma>0$ to be chosen properly.
elements.sent-2411-066: Hence, $\left( \ref{181}\right) $ takes the form \begin{equation}Q(t)\geq F(t)\left\{ -E\left( t\right) \left[ \eta-4\lambda(\sigma +1)\left( 1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right) \right] -2b(\sigma+1)\left( 1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right) \right\} ,\ \ \forall\varepsilon>0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: At this point we may choose $\eta$ more large, if needed, that makes \begin{equation}\eta-4\lambda(\sigma+1)\left( 1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right) =k>0\text{.} \label{182}\end{equation} Finally, as $-kE\left( 0\right) >0,$ we pick $b>0$ small enough so that \begin{equation}-kE\left( 0\right) -2b(\sigma+1)\left( 1+\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\right) \geq0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: Therefore, \begin{equation}Q(t)\geq0, \ \ \ \forall t\geq0\end{equation} and hence \begin{equation}G^{\prime\prime}(t)\leq0,\quad\forall t\geq0;\end{equation} that is $G^{\prime}$ is decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-069: By choosing $t_{0 >0$ large enough we get \begin{equation}F^{\prime}(0)=\int_{0}^{L}(\upsilon_{0}\upsilon_{1}+p_{0}p_{1})dx+bt_{0}>0,\end{equation} hence $G^{\prime (0)<0.$ Finally Taylor expansion of $G$ yields \begin{equation}G(t)\leq G(0)+tG^{\prime}(0),\quad\forall t\geq0,\end{equation} which shows that $G(t)$ vanishes at a time $t_{m}\leq-\frac{G(0) {G^{\prime (0) .$ Consequently $F(t)$ must become infinite at time $t_{m .$ Therefore, the solution $(\upsilon,p)$ blows up with time bounded above by $t_{m .$.}}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-004: Lower bound for the blow-up time
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we estimate a lower bound for the blow-up time.
elements.sent-2411-071: For that reason, we define \begin{equation}\psi(t)=\int_{0}^{L}I(\upsilon,p)dx \label{183}\end{equation} and have the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-072: Assume that $(\upsilon,p)$ is a solution of $\left( \ref{P}\right) $\ that satisfying (G1)-(G3) and does not exist after a time $T^{\ast}$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Then we have \begin{equation}\int_{\psi(0)}^{\infty}\frac{c dy}{y+y^{\beta_{1}}+y^{\beta_{2} }+y^{\beta_{3}}+y^{\beta_{4}}}\leq T^{\ast},\end{equation} for some constant $c>0.$.
elements.sent-2411-074: A direct differentiation of $\psi(t)$ gives \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t)=\int_{0}^{L}\left[ \upsilon_{t}I_{\upsilon}(\upsilon ,p)+p_{t}I_{p}(\upsilon,p)\right] dx.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: Using Young's inequality and (G1), we have \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t) \leq\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2} +p_{t}^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( I_{\upsilon}^{2} +I_{p}^{2}\right) dx\nonumber =\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2}+p_{t}^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( f_{1}^{2}+f_{2}^{2}\right) dx. \label{19}\end{equation} Using (G3) gives \begin{equation}f_{1}^{2}(\upsilon,p)\leq d^{2}\left( \left\vert \upsilon\right\vert ^{\beta_{1}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{\beta_{2}}\right) ^{2}\leq 2d^{2}\left( \left\vert \upsilon\right\vert ^{2\beta_{1}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{2\beta_{2}}\right)\end{equation} \ \ \ and \ \ \begin{equation}\ \ f_{2}^{2}(\upsilon,p)\leq d^{2}\left( \left\vert \upsilon\right\vert ^{\beta_{3}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{\beta_{4}}\right) ^{2}\leq 2d^{2}\left( \left\vert \upsilon\right\vert ^{2\beta_{3}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{2\beta_{4}}\right) .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-056: \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t) \leq\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2} +p_{t}^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( I_{\upsilon}^{2} +I_{p}^{2}\right) dx\nonumber =\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2}+p_{t}^{2}\right) dx+\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( f_{1}^{2}+f_{2}^{2}\right) dx. \label{19}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-076: Using the embedding and Poincar\`{e 's inequality, we obtain from $\left( \ref{19}\right) ,$ \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t) \leq\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2} +p_{t}^{2}\right) dx+d^{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \left\vert \upsilon\right\vert ^{2\beta_{1}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{2\beta_{2}}+\left\vert \upsilon \right\vert ^{2\beta_{3}}+\left\vert p\right\vert ^{2\beta_{4}}\right) dx \leq\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\left( \upsilon_{t}^{2}+p_{t}^{2}\right) dx +d^{2}\left( C_{p}^{\beta_{1}}||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{1}} +C_{p}^{\beta_{2}}||p_{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{2}}+C_{p}^{\beta_{3}}||\upsilon _{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{3}}+C_{p}^{\beta_{4}}||p_{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{4}}\right) ,\end{equation} where $C_{p}$ is the Poincar\`{e 's constant.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus, for $a=d^{2}\max\left\{ C_{p ^{\beta_{1 ,C_{p ^{\beta_{2 ,C_{p ^{\beta_{3 ,C_{p ^{\beta_{4 }\right\} ,$ we get \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t)\leq\frac{1}{2}\left( \left\Vert \upsilon_{t}\right\Vert _{2}^{2}+\left\Vert p_{t}\right\Vert _{2}^{2}\right) +a\left( ||\upsilon _{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{1}}+||p_{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{2}}+||\upsilon_{x} ||_{2}^{2\beta_{3}}+||p_{x}||_{2}^{2\beta_{4}}\right) . \label{190}\end{equation} Recalling $\left( \ref{6}\right) $ and $\left( \ref{11}\right) $ we infer that \begin{equation}\alpha_{1}||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2}+\beta||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2} ^{2}+\rho||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+\mu||p_{t}||_{2}^{2}\leq2\int_{0} ^{L}I(\upsilon,p)dx. \label{191}\end{equation} If we let $m=\min\left\{ \alpha_{1 ,\beta,\rho,\mu\right\} $ and use $\left( \ref{183}\right) $ then $\left( \ref{191}\right) $ becomes \begin{equation}m\left( ||\upsilon_{x}||_{2}^{2}+||\gamma\upsilon_{x}-p_{x}||_{2} ^{2}+||\upsilon_{t}||_{2}^{2}+||p_{t}||_{2}^{2}\right) \leq2\int_{0} ^{L}I(\upsilon,p)dx=2\psi(t).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: So, each term in $\left( \ref{190}\right) $ is bounded above by $\frac{2 {m}\psi(t).$ Therefore, $\left( \ref{190}\right) $ yields \begin{equation}\psi^{\prime}(t)\leq\frac{2}{m}\psi(t)+a\left( \frac{2}{m}\right) ^{r}\left[ \psi^{\beta_{1}}(t)+\psi^{\beta_{2}}(t)+\psi^{\beta_{3}} (t)+\psi^{\beta_{4}}(t)\right] , \label{20}\end{equation} where $r=\max\left\{ \beta_{1 ,\beta_{2 ,\beta_{3 ,\beta_{4}\right\} .$ If we integrate $\left( \ref{20}\right) $ over $(0,T^{\ast )$ and use the substitution $y=\psi(t),$ then we reach \begin{equation}\int_{\psi(0)}^{\infty}\frac{dy}{\frac{2}{m}y+a\left( \frac{2}{m}\right) ^{r}\left[ y^{\beta_{1}}+y^{\beta_{2}}+y^{\beta_{3}}+y^{\beta_{4}}\right] }\leq T^{\ast}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: In our proof of the blow-up property in theorem 3.1, we assume the domain $\left[ 0,L\right] $ is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-080: We notify here that the same proof is also valid in unbounded domain.
elements.sent-2411-081: Moreover, condition $\left( \ref{182}\right) $ is assumed to be a compitition between the source (assumed $\eta$ large) and the damping (where it may be assumed small by taking $\lambda$ small).
elements.sent-2411-082: Finally, positive initial energy can be considered but with different arrangement.
